title: From Grief to Grit: How One Woman Rebuilt Her Life and Business After Loss
guestBio: Leslie Christen is a powerhouse business owner, mother of two boys, and widow who exemplifies resilience in leadership. As one of the original founding members of the Office Hours Girls Club and a longtime friend of host Aimee Rickabus, Leslie has transformed personal tragedy into entrepreneurial strength while navigating the invisible labor of motherhood and business ownership.
excerpt: Join Aimee and longtime friend Leslie Christen for an honest conversation about turning life's deepest challenges into unshakeable strength. From widowhood to entrepreneurship, Leslie shares how she rebuilt her world while managing the invisible labor that holds everything together.
metaDescription: Discover how entrepreneur and widow Leslie Christen transformed grief into grit, rebuilt her business, and leads with heart on The Manage Her podcast.
keyTakeaways: The invisible labor of motherhood becomes even more complex when navigating loss and rebuilding your life as a single parent.
Being underestimated by the patriarchy can actually become a source of strength when you learn to rise above doubt with quiet confidence.
Technology and AI tools can be life-saving resources for busy mothers and business owners juggling multiple responsibilities.
Sisterhood and community support, like the Office Hours Girls Club, provide essential foundations for women entrepreneurs.
Leading with soul and authenticity creates more meaningful connections than trying to fit traditional business molds.
Resilience isn't about being strong all the time—it's about learning to rebuild and adapt when life demands it.
The small rituals and comforts in life become even more important when you're carrying heavy invisible labor.

pullQuotes.text: The invisible labor of holding it all together becomes your superpower when you learn to channel it into leading with heart.

timestamps.label: Life-Saving Tech for Moms & Business Owners
What's Next for Leslie + Favorite Comforts

content:
Some conversations hit you right in the heart, and this episode of The Manage Her is one of them. When Aimee sits down with longtime friend Leslie Christen, what unfolds is a raw, real discussion about what it means to rebuild your entire world while keeping everything else running smoothly—the ultimate invisible labor.

Leslie's story isn't just about business success or motherhood challenges. It's about something deeper: how a woman transforms the unthinkable into unshakeable strength, and how that transformation becomes the foundation for leading others with authenticity and heart.

The Weight of Invisible Labor After Loss

As one of the original founding members of the Office Hours Girls Club, Leslie knows firsthand what it means to carry more than anyone sees. But when life dealt her the devastating blow of widowhood, that invisible labor took on entirely new dimensions. Suddenly, she wasn't just managing the usual juggling act of motherhood and business ownership—she was rebuilding her entire identity while ensuring her two boys never felt the weight of what she was carrying.

This is where Leslie's story becomes so powerful for every woman listening. You don't have to be a widow to understand the crushing responsibility of holding everything together while your own world feels like it's falling apart. Whether you're going through a divorce, dealing with financial stress, managing a health crisis, or simply drowning in the daily demands of being everything to everyone, Leslie's journey shows you that it's possible to not just survive, but to emerge stronger.

The invisible labor that women carry—the mental load, the emotional management, the constant vigilance that keeps families and businesses running—becomes both a burden and a superpower. Leslie learned to channel that natural ability to see everything, manage everything, and anticipate everything into a leadership style that's uniquely feminine and incredibly effective.

Rising Above the Patriarchy with Quiet Confidence

One of the most striking themes in Leslie's story is how being underestimated actually became her advantage. In a world that often dismisses women's capabilities, especially when they're managing grief or major life transitions, Leslie discovered that flying under the radar gave her the space to rebuild on her own terms.

This resonates with so many women who've felt overlooked, undervalued, or written off because of their circumstances. Maybe you've been the woman coming back to work after maternity leave, wondering if anyone takes you seriously anymore. Maybe you've been the entrepreneur pitching to rooms full of men who can't see past your gender or your story. Leslie's experience shows you that their underestimation says nothing about your capabilities and everything about their limitations.

The patriarchy wants women to believe that showing vulnerability is weakness, that admitting struggle disqualifies us from leadership. Leslie's journey proves the opposite. Her willingness to acknowledge her grief, to ask for help when she needed it, and to lead with her whole heart—including the broken parts—made her a more powerful leader, not a weaker one.

Technology as a Lifeline for Modern Women

Let's talk about the practical magic that helps make it all work. Leslie and Aimee dive deep into the tech tools and AI applications that have become genuine lifesavers for busy mothers and business owners. This isn't about becoming more efficient for efficiency's sake—it's about creating breathing room in your life so you can focus on what truly matters.

When you're managing the invisible labor of running a household, raising children, and building a business, every minute saved is a minute you can invest in your own healing, growth, or simply catching your breath. The right technology doesn't replace your intuition or your heart—it amplifies your ability to lead with both.

From scheduling apps that sync your entire family's life to AI tools that handle routine business tasks, these resources aren't luxuries for overwhelmed women—they're necessities. Leslie's openness about leaning on technology shows you that asking for help, even from apps and algorithms, isn't cheating. It's strategic.

The Power of Sisterhood and Community

Perhaps nothing in Leslie's story is more powerful than her connection to the Office Hours Girls Club and the sisterhood that surrounded her during her darkest moments. This community didn't just offer sympathy—they offered practical support, business connections, and the kind of accountability that only comes from women who truly understand the weight you're carrying.

The rituals matter too. Those snack traditions at Office Hours meetings, the text chains that keep everyone connected, the small moments of joy shared between women who get it—these aren't frivolous additions to business networking. They're the foundation that makes everything else possible.

If you're trying to do it all alone, Leslie's story is your permission slip to stop. Find your tribe. Join communities. Show up for other women, and let them show up for you. The myth of the self-made woman is just that—a myth. Every successful woman has a network of other women holding her up, even when it's invisible to the outside world.

Leading with Soul in a Soulless World

What makes Leslie's leadership style so compelling is her refusal to compartmentalize her humanity. She brings her whole self to her business—the grief, the joy, the vulnerability, the strength. In a business world that often rewards emotional detachment and ruthless ambition, Leslie proves that leading with soul isn't just possible, it's powerful.

This is your invitation to stop trying to fit into leadership molds that were never designed for you. Your empathy isn't a weakness—it's your competitive advantage. Your ability to see and care for the whole person, not just the employee or client, creates loyalty that no corporate strategy can manufacture. Your willingness to show up authentically in a world of facades makes you unforgettable.

Leslie's journey from grief to grit isn't about becoming harder or more distant. It's about becoming more herself—more connected to her values, more committed to her vision, more confident in her ability to lead others through their own challenges because she's walked through the fire herself.

As you listen to this conversation, pay attention to the moments when Leslie's voice gets softer, more vulnerable. Those aren't the weak moments—those are the moments when her true strength shows through. That's the kind of leader the world needs more of, and that's the kind of leader you have the power to become.
